cards.text: {{c1::Software testing}} (also called {{c2::software validation}}) is the process of determining whether or not the software actually works as expected.
Testing a small part of your code in isolation to ensure that unit of code is correct is called {{c1::unit testing}}.

cards.front: When should you start testing your code?
cards.back: As soon as you've written a non-trivial function. Test your code as you go, not just at the end.

cards.concept: Why test your program in small pieces rather than all at once
cards.explanation:
Testing small pieces (functions or classes) and integrating them one at a time allows you to:
  • Identify errors immediately when they're introduced
  • Know exactly where to look for the problem (in recently added code)
  • Fix small problems before they become large ones
  • Avoid ripple effects from late-discovered bugs
This is similar to an auto manufacturer testing each component before installing it in the car.
examples: // Good: Test isLowerVowel() immediately after writing it
// Test right away, then integrate into larger program

cards.text: Once each unit has been tested in isolation, they can be integrated into your program and retested to make sure they were integrated properly. This is called {{c1::integration testing}}.

cards.question: What does this automated test function return if all tests pass?
cards.explanation: The function returns 0 if all tests passed, or the test number (1, 2, etc.) that failed. Returning 0 signals success in this pattern.

cards.text: A better method for automated testing is to use {{c1::assert}}, which will cause the program to {{c2::abort}} with an error message if any test fails.

cards.front: What are the two main advantages of preserving your tests in a separate test function instead of deleting temporary test code?
cards.back:
1. You can re-run the tests later to ensure you haven't broken anything when modifying code
2. You don't have to remember what the expected outputs should be

cards.concept: The three approaches to testing code in units
cards.explanation:
There are three common approaches for unit testing:
  • Informal testing: Write temporary test code, run it, then erase it
  • Preserving tests: Move test code into a test function you can call later
  • Automated testing: Write tests that check results automatically and return pass/fail status
Each approach has trade-offs between convenience and maintainability.
examples: // Automated: int testVowel() { if (!isLowerVowel('a')) return 1; return 0; }

cards.front: What is a unit testing framework?
cards.back: A third-party framework designed to help simplify the process of writing, maintaining, and executing unit tests. These frameworks provide consistent structure for organizing tests.

cards.front: Why shouldn't you test every possible combination of inputs for a function that takes two 4-byte integers?
cards.back: It would require approximately 18 quintillion tests (2^64 combinations). You should instead test representative cases from each category (e.g., x > y, x < y, x == y).

cards.text: Best practice: Write your program in {{c1::small, well defined units}} (functions or classes), {{c2::compile often}}, and {{c3::test your code as you go}}.
